questions.question: The Spanish-American War of 1898 resulted in the US acquiring which overseas territories from Spain?
options: Cuba and Jamaica
Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
The Virgin Islands and Panama
Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Dominican Republic
questions.answer: Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
questions.explanation: The Spanish-American War (April-August 1898) was triggered by the explosion of the USS Maine in Havana harbor and US interest in Cuba's independence struggle. The short war ended with the Treaty of Paris: Spain ceded Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines to the US ($20 million payment for the Philippines). Cuba became nominally independent but under US influence. The war marked America's emergence as an imperial power and the end of Spain's colonial empire. Theodore Roosevelt led the 'Rough Riders' in Cuba.
questions.memory_hook: The Spanish-American War lasted 113 days and made the US an empire — gaining Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines for $20 million. Cuba got nominal independence but remained a US protectorate. Teddy Roosevelt became famous charging San Juan Hill.

questions.question: The Nuremberg Trials (1945-1946) were landmark international tribunals held after WWII. What legal principle did they establish that was unprecedented?
options: That soldiers must follow orders from commanding officers regardless of consequences
That individuals could be held criminally responsible for crimes against humanity, even when acting under orders or as agents of a state
That war reparations must be paid to victorious nations by defeated governments
That occupied territories must hold elections within 5 years of liberation
questions.answer: That individuals could be held criminally responsible for crimes against humanity, even when acting under orders or as agents of a state
questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Trials prosecuted 24 major Nazi war criminals for crimes against peace, war crimes, and crimes against humanity. The landmark principle: 'following orders' is not a defense for committing atrocities. This established individual criminal responsibility under international law. Out of 24 defendants: 12 received death sentences, 7 imprisonment, 3 acquitted. The trials led to the Genocide Convention (1948), Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), and eventually the International Criminal Court.
questions.memory_hook: Nuremberg established that 'I was following orders' is not a defense for atrocities — a revolutionary legal principle. It created the framework for all subsequent international war crimes tribunals, from Yugoslavia to Rwanda to the ICC.

questions.question: The Ottoman Empire, which lasted over 600 years, formally ended in 1922. What nation state replaced it?
options: The Republic of Turkey, founded by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
The Kingdom of Arabia, under the Hashemite dynasty
The Byzantine Empire's restoration
The Ottoman Republic under the last Sultan's descendants
questions.answer: The Republic of Turkey, founded by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
questions.explanation: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk led the Turkish War of Independence (1919-1923) after the Ottoman Empire's defeat in WWI and the attempted partition of Anatolia. The Republic of Turkey was proclaimed on October 29, 1923. Atatürk ('Father of Turks') rapidly modernized Turkey: abolished the caliphate, replaced Arabic script with Latin alphabet, secularized courts and education, and gave women the right to vote (1934). The abolition of the Sultanate (1922) and Caliphate (1924) ended institutions that had existed for centuries.
questions.memory_hook: Atatürk transformed what remained of the Ottoman Empire into a secular modern republic in barely a decade — switching the alphabet, abolishing religious courts, and giving women the vote faster than most European democracies.

questions.question: In American football, a quarterback's passer rating (or QBR) measures passing performance. Who holds the NFL record for the highest single-season passer rating?
options: Joe Montana (1989)
Peyton Manning (2004)
Aaron Rodgers (2011)
Patrick Mahomes (2018)
questions.explanation: Aaron Rodgers set the NFL record for single-season passer rating in 2011 with a rating of 122.5, throwing 45 touchdowns with only 6 interceptions. The passer rating formula considers completions, yards, touchdowns, and interceptions relative to attempts. Rodgers is known for his accuracy — he led the NFL in passer rating for multiple seasons. He won Super Bowl XLV with the Green Bay Packers following the 2010 season.
questions.memory_hook: Aaron Rodgers's 122.5 passer rating in 2011 remains the NFL record — 45 TDs, only 6 interceptions. The passer rating formula rewards accuracy, not just volume, making this one of the most efficient passing seasons ever.

questions.question: The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens in 1896. How many nations participated, and which country won the most gold medals?
options: 14 nations; Greece
30 nations; United States
57 nations; Great Britain
14 nations; United States
questions.explanation: The 1896 Athens Olympics had 14 participating nations and 241 athletes (all male). The United States won 11 gold medals (most), followed by Greece with 10 and Germany with 6. The US track and field team dominated. American James Connolly became the first modern Olympic champion (triple jump). The marathon was won by Greek shepherd Spiridon Louis, making him a national hero. The Olympics were revived by French educator Pierre de Coubertin.
questions.memory_hook: The 1896 Athens Olympics had 14 nations and 241 athletes — all men. The US won the most golds despite being the furthest away. Greek shepherd Spiridon Louis won the marathon and became a national hero overnight.

questions.question: Mozart composed his first symphony at age 8 and died at 35. Which of his works premiered just two months before his death and remained unfinished?
options: Don Giovanni
The Magic Flute
Requiem in D Minor
Symphony No. 41 (Jupiter)
questions.explanation: Mozart's Requiem in D Minor was commissioned anonymously in 1791 (later revealed to be Count Franz von Walsegg). Mozart died on December 5, 1791, leaving the Requiem unfinished — only the Introitus was completed in full by Mozart; most movements were sketched. His pupil Franz Xaver Süssmayr completed it. The mystery of the anonymous commission, combined with Mozart's illness and the work's funereal nature, created enduring legends about Mozart 'composing his own requiem.' The film Amadeus (1984) dramatized this.
questions.memory_hook: Mozart died at 35 while composing his own funeral mass — someone anonymously commissioned a Requiem, and Mozart became convinced it was for himself. He was right. His student finished it after Mozart's death.

questions.question: 'Hamlet' is Shakespeare's longest play. What is the most famous soliloquy in the play, and what philosophical question does it pose?
options: 'Friends, Romans, countrymen' — questioning loyalty after Caesar's murder
'To be or not to be' — contemplating whether it is better to endure life's suffering or to end one's life
'All the world's a stage' — examining the seven stages of human life
'O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo' — questioning the meaning of names and identity
questions.answer: 'To be or not to be' — contemplating whether it is better to endure life's suffering or to end one's life
questions.explanation: Hamlet's 'To be or not to be' (Act 3, Scene 1) is the most quoted speech in English literature. Hamlet contemplates whether it is nobler to suffer life's injustices ('slings and arrows of outrageous fortune') or to act against them — or to end one's existence, except that the 'undiscovered country' of death (from which no traveler returns) makes us bear known troubles rather than risk unknown ones. The speech reflects Hamlet's paralysis throughout the play: he cannot decide whether to avenge his father's murder.
questions.memory_hook: 'To be or not to be' is about inaction — Hamlet can't decide to kill Claudius or give up. The fear of death's 'undiscovered country' paralyzes him. Shakespeare captured the universal experience of choosing suffering you know over unknowns you don't.

questions.question: The novel 'Don Quixote' (1605) by Miguel de Cervantes is often called the first modern novel. What is its central conceit?
options: A Portuguese explorer imagines himself discovering a new world that already has inhabitants
A Spanish nobleman reads so many chivalric romances that he loses his mind and believes himself to be a knight errant
A medieval merchant trades along the Silk Road and mistakes foreign customs for magic
A Spanish Inquisitor secretly doubts the faith he enforces and records his questions in a private journal
questions.answer: A Spanish nobleman reads so many chivalric romances that he loses his mind and believes himself to be a knight errant
questions.explanation: Alonso Quijano, an aging Spanish nobleman, becomes obsessed with chivalric romances and renames himself 'Don Quixote de la Mancha,' setting out as a knight errant with his squire Sancho Panza. He mistakes windmills for giants, inns for castles, and peasant women for noble ladies. Cervantes uses this conceit to satirize the popular romance novels of the time while exploring themes of reality vs. illusion, madness vs. idealism, and heroism. 'Tilting at windmills' entered language as a phrase from this book.
questions.memory_hook: Don Quixote is the original 'the call is coming from inside the house' story — a man driven mad by reading too many fantasy novels who can no longer distinguish fiction from reality. Cervantes wrote a satire that accidentally became the model for all subsequent novels.

questions.question: The Yellow River (Huang He) is China's second-longest river and the cradle of Chinese civilization. Why is it called 'China's Sorrow'?
options: Because it runs through historically impoverished provinces
Because of its devastating floods — it has changed course 26 times and caused some of history's deadliest floods, killing millions
Because it carries industrial pollution that has killed most aquatic life
Because ancient Chinese armies were repeatedly defeated at river crossings by invaders
questions.answer: Because of its devastating floods — it has changed course 26 times and caused some of history's deadliest floods, killing millions
questions.explanation: The Yellow River (named for the yellow loess silt it carries) has flooded catastrophically throughout history, earning the name 'China's Sorrow.' The 1887 flood killed 900,000-2 million people. The 1931 flood killed 1-4 million (estimates vary). The river has changed its lower course 26 times in recorded history — sometimes shifting its outlet to the Yellow Sea hundreds of kilometers north or south. Despite this, Chinese civilization developed along it because the silt deposits create fertile farmland.
questions.memory_hook: The Yellow River built Chinese civilization on its silt — then periodically flooded and killed millions. The 1887 flood killed up to 2 million people. It has changed its course 26 times; the gift and the curse are the same river.

questions.question: The Baltic Sea is a nearly landlocked sea in Northern Europe. What makes its water unusual compared to other seas?
options: It is warmer than other Northern European waters due to undersea volcanic activity
It has very low salinity — much less salty than ocean water — because many rivers drain into it and its connection to the North Sea is very narrow
It is entirely fresh water, not salt water, making it technically a lake
It is unusually deep, reaching over 5,000 meters in places
questions.answer: It has very low salinity — much less salty than ocean water — because many rivers drain into it and its connection to the North Sea is very narrow
questions.explanation: The Baltic Sea has the lowest salinity of any sea — only 0.5-0.8% salt (ocean average is 3.5%). This is because over 250 rivers drain into it, bringing freshwater, while its only connection to saline water is the narrow straits between Denmark and Sweden. The low salinity means the Baltic freezes more easily in winter and has unique ecosystems adapted to brackish water. The Baltic is also shallow (average 55m) and nearly enclosed.
questions.memory_hook: The Baltic Sea is so diluted by river runoff that it's barely salty — 0.5-0.8% vs 3.5% for normal ocean water. Its unique brackish water creates ecosystems that exist nowhere else, and it freezes most winters.

questions.question: The Sun orbits the center of the Milky Way galaxy — a journey called a 'galactic year' or 'cosmic year.' How long does one galactic year take, and what does it reveal about the solar system's age?
options: One galactic year is approximately 250,000 years; the solar system is about 46 galactic years old, meaning it was born when the Milky Way was roughly half its current age

One galactic year is approximately 225–250 million years; the solar system is about 18 galactic years old, meaning the last time Earth was in its current position in the galaxy, it was the Triassic period
One galactic year is approximately 1 billion years; the solar system is about 4.6 galactic years old, making it a relatively young system that has barely begun its galactic journey
One galactic year is approximately 1,000 years because the galaxy rotates extremely fast at its outer rim — like water in a centrifuge — with all stars orbiting in precisely 986 Earth years
questions.answer: One galactic year is approximately 225–250 million years; the solar system is about 18 galactic years old, meaning the last time Earth was in its current position in the galaxy, it was the Triassic period
questions.explanation: The Sun orbits at about 220 km/s and is located about 26,000 light-years from the galactic center. One complete orbit takes 225–250 million years. The solar system is 4.6 billion years old ÷ 225 million = roughly 18–20 galactic years old. 225 million years ago, Earth was in the Triassic period and dinosaurs were just beginning to appear. One galactic year from now, whatever life exists on Earth will be in a completely different region of the Milky Way. The Milky Way itself is about 50–60 galactic years old.
questions.memory_hook: One galactic year = 225–250 million Earth years; the Sun is about 18–20 galactic years old. When Earth was last in its current galactic position, it was the Triassic — early dinosaurs were just emerging. The Milky Way itself is only about 50-60 galactic years old.

questions.question: What is DNA replication, and why must it occur before cell division?
options: DNA replication is the process of converting DNA into proteins; it occurs after cell division to replace proteins
DNA replication duplicates the entire genome before cell division, ensuring each daughter cell receives a complete set of genetic instructions. Each strand of the double helix serves as a template
DNA replication only occurs in stem cells and cancer cells; most cells don't replicate their DNA
DNA replication is the same as DNA transcription — both create RNA from a DNA template
questions.answer: DNA replication duplicates the entire genome before cell division, ensuring each daughter cell receives a complete set of genetic instructions. Each strand of the double helix serves as a template
questions.explanation: Before a cell divides, it must copy its ~3 billion base pairs of DNA so each daughter cell gets a complete genome. The double helix unwinds; each strand acts as a template; DNA polymerase adds complementary bases. The result: two identical double helices from one. The process is remarkably accurate — one error per 10⁹ bases copied — thanks to proofreading enzymes. Errors that slip through are mutations. Errors in DNA replication in rapidly dividing cells are a primary cause of cancer.
questions.memory_hook: DNA replication copies 3 billion base pairs with an error rate of one mistake per billion bases — better accuracy than any human-made copying technology. The errors that slip through become mutations; accumulated mutations in dividing cells lead to cancer.
